subsection.title: Statement
subsection.p: More generally, if the relevant higher partial derivatives are continuous, then the order of differentiation can be switched.

md.mrow: 1. \amp f_{xy}\text{ and }f_{yx}\text{ exist near the point.}
2. \amp f_{xy}\text{ and }f_{yx}\text{ are continuous near the point.}

subsection.title: Proof idea
subsection.p: Measure the net change of f around a small rectangle: [a,a+h]\times[b,b+k].
First, take x-changes, then y-changes. This connects \Delta to f_{xy}.
Second, take y-changes, then x-changes. This connects \Delta to f_{yx}.
Both computations measure the same corner difference. After division by hk and letting h,k\to0, continuity forces f_{xy}(a,b)=f_{yx}(a,b).

subsection.title: What can fail
subsection.p: If the mixed partials are not continuous, they can differ.

subsection.title: Moral
subsection.p: The equality of mixed partials is not only about the symbols xy and yx. It is about enough smoothness to make the small-rectangle measurement independent of the order in which it is taken.
